name: Sugar Free Kheer
preparation: Wash and soak rice for 30 minutes; drain.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan; add rice.
Cook on low heat, stirring frequently, for 25-30 minutes until rice is soft and milk thickens.
Add chopped dates and cook for 5 more minutes until dates dissolve and sweeten the kheer.
Add cardamom powder and saffron; mix well.
Garnish with sliced almonds and pistachios.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Sweetened only with dates - no sugar, honey, or jaggery needed.

name: Oats Kheer
preparation: Dry roast oats in a pan for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan.
Add roasted oats and stir well.
Cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes, stirring frequently.
Add jaggery/honey and cardamom powder; mix until dissolved.
Garnish with chopped almonds, cashews, and raisins.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Quick oats kheer ready in 15 minutes - high fiber alternative to rice kheer.

name: Ragi Kheer
preparation: Mix ragi flour with 1/4 cup cold milk to make a smooth paste.
Boil remaining milk in a pan.
Slowly pour the ragi paste into boiling milk, stirring continuously to prevent lumps.
Cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring constantly, until thick.
Add jaggery, cardamom, and saffron; mix well.
Garnish with chopped almonds and raisins.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Ragi is rich in calcium and iron - a nutritious dessert choice.

name: Makhana Kheer
preparation: Heat ghee; roast makhana until crispy and light golden.
Let makhana cool slightly; grind half into coarse powder, keep rest whole.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan; add ground makhana powder.
Cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring frequently.
Add whole roasted makhana, jaggery, and cardamom; mix well.
Garnish with sliced almonds and pistachios.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Makhana kheer is great for fasting days - light yet satisfying.

name: Brown Rice Kheer
preparation: Soak brown rice for at least 1 hour; drain.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan; add soaked rice.
Cook on low heat for 35-40 minutes, stirring frequently, until rice is very soft.
Mash rice slightly with the back of a spoon for creamier texture.
Add jaggery, cardamom, and saffron; mix until dissolved.
Garnish with almonds and raisins.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Brown rice kheer has a nutty flavor and more fiber than regular rice kheer.

name: Quinoa Kheer
preparation: Rinse quinoa thoroughly to remove bitterness.
Boil milk in a pan; add quinoa.
Cook on low heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until quinoa is soft and milk thickens.
Add honey/jaggery, cardamom, and saffron.
Stir well and cook for 2 more minutes.
Garnish with chopped almonds and pistachios.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Quinoa kheer is a complete protein dessert - all essential amino acids included.

name: Bajra Kheer
preparation: Heat ghee in a pan; add bajra flour and roast on low for 3-4 minutes until fragrant.
Boil milk separately.
Slowly add hot milk to the roasted bajra, stirring constantly to prevent lumps.
Cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes until thick and creamy.
Add jaggery and cardamom; mix well.
Garnish with almonds and coconut.
Serve warm.
notes: Bajra kheer is warming and especially good in winter months.

name: Sabudana Kheer Light
preparation: Soak sabudana in water for 2 hours; drain completely.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan.
Add soaked sabudana and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes, stirring frequently.
Cook until sabudana pearls become translucent.
Add sugar/jaggery, cardamom, and saffron; stir well.
Garnish with almonds and cashews.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Light version of sabudana kheer with less sugar than traditional recipe.

name: Vermicelli Kheer Light
preparation: Heat ghee; roast vermicelli on low heat until golden brown.
Boil milk separately.
Add roasted vermicelli to boiling milk.
Cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until vermicelli is soft.
Add sugar/jaggery, cardamom, and saffron; mix well.
Garnish with cashews and raisins.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: A lighter version of seviyan kheer using less ghee and sugar.

name: Carrot Kheer
preparation: Heat ghee; sauté grated carrots for 3-4 minutes.
Add milk and bring to a boil.
Cook on low heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring frequently, until carrots are soft and milk reduces.
Add jaggery/sugar and cardamom; mix well.
Cook for 3-4 more minutes until well blended.
Garnish with almonds and cashews.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Carrot kheer combines the goodness of gajar halwa with the creaminess of kheer.

name: Oats Ladoo
preparation: Dry roast oats on low heat for 5-6 minutes until golden and fragrant.
Let oats cool; grind into a coarse powder.
Heat ghee; add powdered jaggery and melt on low heat.
Add oats powder, coconut, almonds, and cardamom.
Mix well until everything comes together.
Let cool slightly; shape into small round ladoos while warm.
Store in an airtight container.
notes: High-fiber oats ladoos - a guilt-free sweet snack.

name: Date Walnut Ladoo
preparation: Roughly chop dates and walnuts.
Heat ghee in a pan; add dates and cook on low heat for 3-4 minutes until soft and sticky.
Add chopped walnuts and cardamom; mix well.
Let the mixture cool enough to handle.
Grease hands with ghee and shape into round ladoos.
Roll each ladoo in desiccated coconut.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes before serving.
notes: No added sugar needed - dates provide all the natural sweetness.

name: Dry Fruit Ladoo
preparation: Dry roast all nuts separately until lightly golden; let cool.
Grind nuts coarsely in a food processor.
Add dates and pulse until mixture comes together.
Add cardamom and ghee; pulse once more.
Shape into small round ladoos.
Roll in desiccated coconut.
Store in refrigerator for up to 2 weeks.
notes: Packed with omega-3s and healthy fats from mixed nuts.

name: Coconut Ladoo Healthy
ingredients: 1/12 cup condensed milk (or date paste for sugar-free)
preparation: Dry roast coconut on low heat for 2-3 minutes until slightly golden.
Add condensed milk or date paste and mix well on low heat.
Cook for 5-6 minutes, stirring constantly, until mixture thickens and leaves the sides of the pan.
Add cardamom and almonds; mix well.
Let cool enough to handle.
Grease hands and shape into round ladoos.
Let set at room temperature for 30 minutes.
notes: Classic coconut ladoo made healthier with less condensed milk.

name: Almond Ladoo
preparation: Soak almonds in warm water for 1 hour; peel skin off.
Dry roast peeled almonds lightly; let cool.
Grind almonds into a fine powder.
Heat ghee; add jaggery and melt on low heat.
Add almond powder, cardamom, and saffron; mix well.
Cook for 3-4 minutes until mixture binds together.
Shape into ladoos while warm; let cool.
notes: Rich in vitamin E and healthy fats from almonds.

name: Sesame Ladoo Healthy
preparation: Dry roast sesame seeds on low heat until golden and popping; let cool.
Heat ghee; add grated jaggery and melt on low heat until it forms a syrup.
Add roasted sesame seeds, crushed peanuts, and cardamom.
Mix quickly while hot until everything is well combined.
Grease hands with ghee and shape into ladoos immediately.
Let cool completely before storing.
Store in an airtight container.
notes: Til ladoo is rich in calcium and iron - a traditional winter treat.

name: Peanut Ladoo
preparation: Roast peanuts until golden; remove skin by rubbing in a cloth.
Coarsely grind peanuts in a mixer (not too fine).
Heat ghee; add powdered jaggery and melt on low heat.
Add ground peanuts and cardamom; mix well.
While still warm, shape into round ladoos.
Let cool completely.
Store in an airtight container for up to a week.
notes: Affordable and protein-rich peanut ladoos with jaggery.

name: Ragi Ladoo
preparation: Heat ghee in a pan; add ragi flour.
Roast on low heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring constantly, until aromatic and color darkens slightly.
Let cool for 5 minutes.
Add powdered jaggery, coconut, almonds, and cardamom.
Mix well until everything binds together.
Shape into round ladoos while still slightly warm.
Store in an airtight container.
notes: Ragi ladoos are calcium-rich and perfect for growing children.

name: Nachni Ladoo
preparation: Heat ghee; roast nachni flour on low for 8-10 minutes until fragrant.
Remove from heat and let cool slightly.
Add powdered jaggery, grated coconut, cashews, cardamom, and nutmeg.
Mix thoroughly until mixture holds shape when pressed.
Shape into compact round ladoos.
Let cool completely at room temperature.
Store in an airtight jar.
notes: Nachni is another name for ragi - this version has a hint of nutmeg.

name: Bajra Ladoo
preparation: Heat ghee; roast bajra flour on low heat for 8-10 minutes until deep brown and aromatic.
Remove from heat; let cool for 5 minutes.
Add grated jaggery while still slightly warm (jaggery will melt from residual heat).
Add coconut, almonds, and cardamom; mix well.
Shape into ladoos while warm.
Let cool completely before storing.
Best consumed within a week.
notes: Bajra ladoos are warming and traditionally eaten in winter for energy.

name: Date Nut Barfi
preparation: Heat ghee; add chopped dates and cook on low for 5-7 minutes until soft and sticky.
Add all chopped nuts and cardamom; mix well.
Continue cooking for 3-4 minutes until mixture pulls together.
Grease a plate or tray with ghee.
Spread the mixture evenly, about 1/2 inch thick.
Let cool completely; cut into diamond or square shapes.
Garnish with silver vark if desired.
notes: No added sugar - dates provide natural sweetness and binding.

name: Coconut Barfi Healthy
preparation: Cook grated coconut and milk together on low heat.
Add jaggery/sugar; stir well until dissolved.
Cook for 12-15 minutes, stirring constantly, until mixture thickens and starts leaving the sides.
Add cardamom; mix well.
Grease a plate with ghee; spread the mixture evenly.
Garnish with chopped pistachios.
Let cool completely; cut into squares.
notes: Fresh coconut barfi tastes far better than store-bought versions.

name: Cashew Barfi Light
preparation: Grind cashews into a fine powder.
Make sugar syrup: heat sugar and water until one-string consistency.
Add cashew powder to the syrup; stir continuously on low heat.
Add cardamom and saffron; mix well.
Cook for 5-7 minutes until mixture leaves the sides of the pan.
Grease a plate with ghee; spread mixture and flatten evenly.
Let cool; cut into diamond shapes.
notes: Lighter cashew barfi using less sugar than the traditional recipe.

name: Pista Barfi Light
preparation: Blanch pistachios in hot water for 5 minutes; peel and dry.
Grind pistachios into a fine powder.
Heat milk and sugar; stir until sugar dissolves.
Add pistachio powder and cook on low heat, stirring continuously.
Cook for 8-10 minutes until mixture thickens and leaves the pan.
Add cardamom; spread on a greased plate.
Garnish with saffron; let cool and cut into pieces.
notes: Vibrant green pista barfi - lighter version with reduced sugar.

name: Almond Barfi
preparation: Soak almonds in warm water; peel off skin.
Grind blanched almonds into a smooth paste with milk.
Cook almond paste with sugar on low heat, stirring constantly.
Add cardamom and saffron.
Cook for 10-12 minutes until mixture thickens and pulls away from pan.
Spread on a greased plate; garnish with silver vark.
Let cool completely; cut into squares.
notes: Rich badam barfi made with real almond paste, not almond flour.

name: Ragi Halwa
preparation: Mix ragi flour with 1/2 cup water to make a smooth paste.
Dissolve jaggery in remaining 1/2 cup warm water; strain.
Heat ghee; fry cashews and raisins until golden. Set aside.
Pour ragi paste into the pan; cook on low, stirring constantly.
Add jaggery water gradually, stirring to prevent lumps.
Cook for 8-10 minutes until halwa thickens and ghee separates.
Garnish with fried cashews and raisins.
notes: Nutritious ragi halwa with jaggery - rich in calcium.

name: Carrot Halwa Light
preparation: Heat ghee; add grated carrots and sauté for 5 minutes.
Add milk and cook on medium heat, stirring frequently.
Cook for 20-25 minutes until milk is fully absorbed.
Add jaggery/sugar and cardamom; mix well.
Continue cooking for 5 more minutes until halwa reaches desired consistency.
Garnish with chopped almonds and cashews.
Serve warm.
notes: Light gajar halwa using less ghee and jaggery instead of sugar.

name: Bottle Gourd Halwa Light
ingredients: 2/3 cups bottle gourd (lauki), peeled and grated
preparation: Squeeze excess water from grated lauki.
Heat ghee; sauté grated lauki for 5-6 minutes until water evaporates.
Add milk; cook on medium heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring frequently.
Cook until milk is fully absorbed and lauki turns soft.
Add jaggery/sugar and cardamom; mix well.
Cook for 5 more minutes until halwa thickens.
Garnish with almonds and cashews.
notes: Lauki halwa is lighter than carrot halwa and great for digestion.

name: Pumpkin Halwa
preparation: Heat ghee; add grated pumpkin and sauté for 5 minutes.
Add milk and cook on medium heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring often.
Cook until pumpkin is soft and milk is absorbed.
Add jaggery, cardamom, and saffron; stir well.
Cook for 5 more minutes until halwa is thick and glossy.
Garnish with sliced almonds.
Serve warm.
notes: Kaddu ka halwa is naturally sweet and needs very little added sweetener.

name: Beetroot Halwa
preparation: Heat ghee; add grated beetroot and sauté for 5 minutes.
Add milk; cook on medium heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring frequently.
Cook until milk is absorbed and beetroot is tender.
Add sugar/jaggery and cardamom; mix well.
Continue cooking for 5 minutes until halwa is thick.
Garnish with chopped almonds and pistachios.
Serve warm or cold.
notes: Vibrant beetroot halwa is packed with antioxidants and iron.

name: Moong Dal Halwa Light
preparation: Soak moong dal for 2 hours; drain and grind into coarse paste.
Heat ghee; add moong dal paste and roast on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring constantly.
Roast until golden brown and aromatic.
Add warm milk gradually, stirring to prevent lumps.
Add jaggery/sugar, cardamom, and saffron.
Cook for 8-10 minutes until halwa is thick and ghee separates.
Garnish with almonds and cashews.
notes: Light moong dal halwa using less ghee than traditional recipe.

name: Sweet Potato Halwa
preparation: Boil or steam sweet potato until soft; peel and mash or grate.
Heat ghee; add grated sweet potato and sauté for 3-4 minutes.
Add milk and cook on low heat for 15 minutes, stirring frequently.
Add jaggery and cardamom; mix well.
Cook for 5 more minutes until halwa thickens and becomes glossy.
Garnish with cashews and raisins.
Serve warm.
notes: Sweet potato naturally reduces the need for added sugar.

name: Apple Halwa
preparation: Peel and grate apples.
Heat ghee; add grated apples and sauté for 5-6 minutes until soft.
Add milk and cook on low heat for 10 minutes.
Add jaggery/sugar, cardamom, and cinnamon; mix well.
Cook until halwa is thick and apples are fully cooked.
Garnish with chopped almonds.
Serve warm.
notes: Apple halwa with cinnamon is a comforting, healthy dessert.

name: Papaya Halwa
preparation: Peel and grate ripe papaya.
Heat ghee; add grated papaya and sauté for 5 minutes until water evaporates.
Add milk and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes.
Add sugar/jaggery and cardamom; mix well.
Cook until halwa thickens to desired consistency.
Garnish with cashews and raisins.
Serve warm.
notes: Papaya halwa is naturally sweet and easy to digest.

name: Banana Halwa
preparation: Peel and mash bananas well.
Heat ghee in a pan; add mashed bananas.
Cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring constantly.
Add sugar/jaggery and cardamom; mix well.
Continue cooking until halwa becomes thick and glossy.
Garnish with cashews and raisins.
Serve warm.
notes: Ripe bananas add natural sweetness - reduce sugar accordingly.

name: Fruit Sandesh
ingredients: 1/4 cup fresh chhena (paneer from 1 litre milk)
preparation: Knead fresh chhena on a smooth surface for 8-10 minutes until very smooth.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom; knead well.
Divide into two portions; mix fruit pulp into one portion.
Shape into small discs or use molds.
Create layered or swirled sandesh with plain and fruit portions.
Garnish with chopped pistachios.
Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
notes: Bengali sandesh with fruit - light and delicate dessert.

name: Mango Sandesh
ingredients: 3/4 tbsp mango pulp (from ripe Alphonso mango)
preparation: Knead fresh chhena for 8-10 minutes until silky smooth.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom; knead until combined.
Add mango pulp and mix evenly.
Shape into small flat discs or use sandesh molds.
Garnish with chopped pistachios.
Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
Serve chilled.
notes: Best made during mango season with ripe Alphonso mangoes.

name: Orange Sandesh
preparation: Knead fresh chhena for 8-10 minutes until very smooth.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and orange zest; knead well.
Add orange juice gradually; mix until well combined.
Shape into small flat discs or rounds.
Garnish each piece with a tiny strip of orange peel.
Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
Serve chilled.
notes: Citrusy orange sandesh - a refreshing twist on the Bengali classic.

name: Pineapple Sandesh
ingredients: 3/4 tbsp pineapple, finely crushed and drained
preparation: Crush pineapple finely and squeeze out excess juice.
Knead fresh chhena for 8-10 minutes until smooth.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom; knead well.
Add drained pineapple and mix gently.
Shape into small discs or rolls.
Garnish with pistachios.
Refrigerate and serve chilled.
notes: Ensure pineapple is well drained to prevent sandesh from becoming watery.

name: Date Sandesh
ingredients: 1/16 cup dates, deseeded and finely chopped
preparation: Cook chopped dates in 2 tbsp water until soft and paste-like.
Knead fresh chhena for 8-10 minutes until smooth.
Add date paste and cardamom; knead until evenly mixed.
Add sugar only if needed - dates may be sweet enough.
Shape into small round discs.
Garnish with chopped almonds.
Refrigerate and serve chilled.
notes: Date sandesh is naturally sweet and refined sugar-free.

name: Mishti Doi Light
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to 1.5 cups on medium heat, stirring occasionally.
Add jaggery/sugar and stir until dissolved.
Let milk cool to lukewarm (just warm to touch).
Mix in curd starter, cardamom, and saffron.
Pour into earthen pots or glass bowls.
Cover and keep in a warm place for 6-8 hours to set.
Refrigerate once set; serve chilled.
notes: Bengali mishti doi is best set in earthen pots for authentic flavor.

name: Low Fat Shrikhand
ingredients: Pinch of saffron, soaked in 1 tsp warm milk
preparation: Hang curd in a muslin cloth overnight to remove all whey.
Transfer thick hung curd (chakka) to a bowl.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and saffron milk.
Whisk until smooth and creamy.
Garnish with chopped pistachios and sliced almonds.
Refrigerate for 1-2 hours before serving.
Serve chilled.
notes: Low-fat shrikhand made with hung curd - all the flavor, less guilt.

name: Mango Shrikhand Light
preparation: Hang curd in muslin cloth overnight; collect thick hung curd.
Add mango pulp, sugar, and cardamom to hung curd.
Whisk until smooth and creamy.
Taste and adjust sweetness if needed.
Garnish with chopped pistachios.
Refrigerate for 2 hours.
Serve chilled in small bowls.
notes: Amrakhand (mango shrikhand) is best in summer with Alphonso mangoes.

name: Kesar Shrikhand Light
ingredients: Generous pinch of saffron, soaked in 2 tsp warm milk
preparation: Hang curd overnight in muslin cloth to get thick chakka.
Soak saffron in warm milk for 10 minutes.
Add sugar, saffron milk, and cardamom to hung curd.
Whisk vigorously until silky smooth.
Garnish with sliced almonds and pistachios.
Refrigerate for 2 hours for flavors to develop.
Serve chilled.
notes: Kesar (saffron) shrikhand is the most popular variety - aromatic and rich.

name: Makhana Barfi
preparation: Roast makhana in ghee until crispy; let cool.
Grind roasted makhana into a fine powder.
Heat milk with sugar; stir until sugar dissolves.
Add makhana powder and cook on low, stirring continuously.
Add cardamom; cook until mixture thickens and leaves the pan.
Spread on a greased plate; garnish with pistachios.
Let cool completely; cut into squares.
notes: Makhana barfi is low in calories and suitable for fasting.

name: Fox Nut Ladoo
preparation: Heat ghee; roast makhana until crispy and golden. Let cool.
Grind roasted makhana into a coarse powder.
In the same pan, add grated jaggery and 1 tsp water; melt into syrup.
Add makhana powder, coconut, almonds, and cardamom.
Mix quickly and shape into ladoos while warm.
Let cool completely.
Store in an airtight container.
notes: Fox nut (makhana) ladoos are light and great for Navratri fasting.

name: Makhana Pudding
preparation: Roast makhana in ghee until crispy.
Grind half the makhana into powder; keep rest whole.
Boil milk; add makhana powder and stir well.
Cook on low heat for 15 minutes, stirring frequently.
Add whole makhana, sugar/honey, cardamom, and saffron.
Cook for 5 more minutes until pudding-like consistency.
Garnish with almonds; serve warm or chilled.
notes: Creamy makhana pudding - a cross between kheer and Western pudding.

name: Phirni Light
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes; drain and grind into a coarse paste.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan.
Add rice paste, stirring continuously to avoid lumps.
Cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes until mixture thickens.
Add sugar, cardamom, and saffron; stir well.
Pour into earthen bowls (shikoras).
Garnish with almonds, pistachios, and rose petals; chill before serving.
notes: Traditional phirni is set in clay bowls which add an earthy flavor.

name: Badam Phirni
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes; soak almonds separately.
Grind rice and almonds together into a smooth paste with 2 tbsp milk.
Boil remaining milk.
Add almond-rice paste, stirring continuously.
Cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes until thick and creamy.
Add sugar, cardamom, and saffron; stir well.
Pour into bowls; garnish with pistachios. Serve chilled.
notes: Almond phirni is extra rich and creamy compared to regular phirni.

name: Mango Phirni
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes; grind into coarse paste.
Boil milk; add rice paste, stirring constantly.
Cook on low heat for 15 minutes until thick.
Add sugar and cardamom; mix well.
Let cool to room temperature.
Fold in mango pulp gently.
Pour into bowls; garnish with pistachios and chill before serving.
notes: Mango phirni is a summer-special variation of the Mughlai classic.

name: Aam Shrikhand
preparation: Hang fresh yogurt in a muslin cloth for 4-6 hours until thick and creamy.
Whisk the hung curd until smooth with no lumps.
Add fresh mango pulp, powdered sugar, cardamom powder, and saffron strands.
Mix well until everything is evenly combined.
Refrigerate for at least 2 hours and serve chilled.
notes: Use Alphonso or Kesar mangoes for the best flavor and color.

name: Rose Shrikhand
preparation: Strain fresh yogurt in a muslin cloth for 4-6 hours to get thick hung curd.
Whisk the hung curd until silky smooth.
Add powdered sugar, rose water, and cardamom powder; mix well.
Garnish with dried rose petals.
Chill in the refrigerator for 2 hours before serving.
notes: Use food-grade rose water for the best aroma; add gradually to taste.

name: Fruit Shrikhand
ingredients: 1/12 cup mixed fruits (pomegranate, grapes, apple), chopped
preparation: Hang yogurt in a muslin cloth for 4-6 hours to get thick hung curd.
Whisk hung curd until smooth and creamy.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and saffron soaked in warm milk.
Fold in the chopped mixed fruits gently.
Refrigerate for 2 hours and serve chilled in bowls.
notes: Add fruits just before serving to keep them fresh and crunchy.

name: Banana Sheera Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a pan and roast semolina on low heat until golden and aromatic.
Add mashed bananas and stir well for 2 minutes.
Gradually add warm water, stirring continuously to avoid lumps.
Add sugar and cardamom powder, cook until the sheera thickens and leaves the sides.
Serve warm garnished with sliced almonds.
notes: Use very ripe bananas for natural sweetness, allowing you to reduce sugar.

name: Sooji Halwa Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pan and roast semolina on low flame until golden.
Fry raisins and cashews in the same pan until they puff up.
Carefully add warm water while stirring continuously to prevent lumps.
Add sugar and cardamom powder, stirring until the halwa thickens.
Cook until the mixture leaves the sides of the pan and serve warm.
notes: Roasting sooji slowly on low heat is the key to a rich, nutty flavor.

name: Rava Sheera Light
preparation: Heat ghee and roast rava on low flame until it turns light golden and aromatic.
In a separate pot, warm the milk and water together.
Slowly pour the warm milk-water mixture into the roasted rava, stirring constantly.
Add sugar and cardamom powder; cook on low heat until thick and smooth.
Garnish with sliced almonds and serve warm.
notes: Adding milk gives sheera a creamier texture compared to water-only versions.

name: Coconut Burfi Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a non-stick pan and add desiccated coconut.
Roast on low heat for 2 minutes, stirring continuously.
Add low-fat condensed milk and cardamom powder.
Cook on low flame, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens and pulls away from the pan.
Spread onto a greased plate, let it cool, and cut into diamond shapes.
notes: Use fresh desiccated coconut for a moister, more flavorful burfi.

name: Nariyal Ladoo
preparation: Heat ghee in a pan and add freshly grated coconut.
Roast on low heat for 3-4 minutes until slightly golden.
Add condensed milk and cardamom powder, stirring continuously.
Cook until the mixture becomes thick and starts leaving the pan sides.
Let it cool slightly, then shape into round ladoos while still warm.
notes: Shape the ladoos while the mixture is still warm for the best results.

name: Coconut Date Balls
preparation: Soak dates in warm water for 15 minutes, then drain and blend into a smooth paste.
Mix the date paste with half the desiccated coconut and chopped almonds.
Add cardamom powder and mix well.
Roll into small balls using slightly greased palms.
Roll each ball in the remaining desiccated coconut and refrigerate for 1 hour.
notes: No added sugar needed - dates provide all the natural sweetness.

name: Fig Barfi
preparation: Soak dried figs in warm water for 30 minutes, then drain and grind into a paste.
Chop the mixed nuts finely.
Heat ghee in a pan, add the fig paste and cook on low heat for 5-7 minutes.
Add chopped nuts and cardamom powder, stirring until the mixture thickens.
Spread onto a greased plate, let it set, and cut into squares.
notes: Figs are naturally high in calcium and iron, making this a nutritious sweet.

name: Anjeer Ladoo
preparation: Dry roast almonds lightly and chop them coarsely.
Grind dried figs into a sticky paste in a food processor.
Mix fig paste with chopped almonds, half the coconut, and cardamom.
Heat ghee, add the mixture, and cook for 3-4 minutes on low heat.
Cool slightly, shape into round ladoos, and roll in remaining coconut.
notes: These ladoos stay fresh for up to 2 weeks when stored in an airtight container.

name: Anjeer Roll
preparation: Soak dried figs in warm water for 20 minutes, drain, and grind to a smooth paste.
Finely chop the mixed nuts.
Mix fig paste with chopped nuts and cardamom powder.
Shape the mixture into a log on a sheet of plastic wrap.
Roll the log in desiccated coconut, wrap tightly, and refrigerate for 2 hours. Slice into rounds.
notes: Refrigerate the log well before slicing for clean, even rounds.

name: Gulkand Kulfi Light
preparation: Boil milk in a heavy-bottomed pan and reduce to half on low heat, stirring frequently.
Add sugar and cardamom powder, stir until dissolved.
Remove from heat and let it cool slightly, then mix in gulkand.
Pour into kulfi moulds, add chopped pistachios, and freeze for 6-8 hours.
Unmould by running warm water over the outside of the mould and serve.
notes: Add gulkand after the milk cools slightly to preserve its rose fragrance.

name: Mango Kulfi Light
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to half by simmering on low heat, stirring often.
Add sugar and cardamom, stir until dissolved, then let it cool.
Mix in fresh mango pulp thoroughly.
Pour into kulfi moulds, sprinkle almonds, and freeze for 6-8 hours.
Unmould and serve immediately.
notes: Use Alphonso mango pulp for the richest flavor and vibrant color.

name: Malai Kulfi Light
preparation: Boil milk in a heavy pan and simmer on low heat, stirring frequently.
Add saffron strands and continue reducing until milk is one-third of the original volume.
Add sugar and cardamom powder, stir well.
Let it cool, pour into moulds with chopped nuts, and freeze for 6-8 hours.
Unmould by dipping in warm water and serve garnished with more nuts.
notes: Reducing milk slowly creates the characteristic dense, creamy kulfi texture.

name: Jaggery Payasam
preparation: Wash and soak rice for 30 minutes, then cook in milk on low heat until very soft.
Dissolve grated jaggery in 2 tbsp warm water and strain to remove impurities.
Add jaggery syrup to the cooked rice-milk mixture and stir well.
Add cardamom powder and cook for 5 more minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in a tsp of ghee and add as garnish.
notes: Always add jaggery after the rice is fully cooked, as it can curdle milk if added too early.

name: Gur Ki Kheer
preparation: Wash and soak basmati rice for 30 minutes.
Boil milk, add soaked rice, and cook on low heat until rice is soft and milk thickens.
Dissolve gur in a little warm water and strain.
Add the jaggery liquid to the kheer and stir gently.
Add cardamom and almonds, serve warm or chilled.
notes: Gur gives a distinct caramel-like flavor that white sugar cannot replicate.

name: Jaggery Rice Pudding
preparation: Wash rice and cook in milk on low heat, stirring often, until the rice is very soft.
Melt jaggery in 2 tbsp warm water, strain, and add to the pudding.
Add cardamom powder and raisins.
Cook for another 5 minutes until well combined and thick.
Serve warm or chilled as preferred.
notes: Short-grain rice releases more starch, giving the pudding a naturally creamier texture.

name: Pumpkin Payasam
preparation: Cook pumpkin cubes in water until very soft, then mash well.
Add light coconut milk to the mashed pumpkin and simmer on low heat.
Dissolve jaggery in 2 tbsp warm water and add to the pumpkin mixture.
Add cardamom powder and cook for 5 more minutes.
Fry cashews in a tsp of ghee and garnish before serving.
notes: Pumpkin is naturally sweet, so adjust jaggery according to the variety used.

name: Ada Pradhaman Light
preparation: Roast ada in a dry pan for 2 minutes, then cook in water until soft. Drain.
Melt jaggery in a little water, strain, and set aside.
Heat coconut milk on low flame, add the jaggery syrup, and stir well.
Add cooked ada and cardamom powder; simmer for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee and garnish. Serve warm.
notes: Ada Pradhaman is a traditional Kerala Onam Sadhya dessert.

name: Palada Payasam Light
preparation: Soak palada in warm water for 15 minutes, then drain.
Boil milk and add the soaked palada.
Cook on low heat, stirring frequently, until the palada is soft and milk thickens.
Add sugar, cardamom powder, and saffron; stir until dissolved.
Simmer for another 5 minutes and serve warm.
notes: Palada Payasam thickens as it cools, so keep it slightly thinner than desired consistency.

name: Rasgulla Light
preparation: Boil milk, add lemon juice to curdle, and strain through muslin to get chhena.
Knead chhena for 8-10 minutes until very smooth and no cracks appear.
Shape into small smooth balls, ensuring no cracks on the surface.
Boil sugar and water to make a light syrup, add cardamom.
Gently add chhena balls to the boiling syrup, cover, and cook for 12-15 minutes until they double in size.
notes: Knead the chhena until it is completely smooth with no graininess for spongy rasgullas.

name: Cham Cham Light
preparation: Curdle milk with lemon juice, strain to get chhena, and knead until smooth.
Shape into small oval/cylindrical pieces.
Boil sugar and water to make syrup, add chhena ovals, and cook for 12 minutes.
Remove and let them cool in the syrup for 30 minutes.
Roll in desiccated coconut mixed with cardamom and serve.
notes: Shape into ovals rather than balls for the authentic Cham Cham look.

name: Pantua Light
preparation: Knead chhena with maida until smooth, shape into small oval balls.
Heat oil and shallow fry the balls on low heat until deep golden brown.
Boil sugar and water with cardamom to make a medium-thick syrup.
Soak the fried pantuas in warm syrup for at least 2 hours.
Serve at room temperature soaked in syrup.
notes: Fry on low heat for even browning; high heat will brown outside but leave the inside raw.

name: Gulab Jamun Baked
preparation: Mix milk powder, whole wheat flour, and ghee. Add milk gradually to form a soft dough.
Shape into small smooth balls without any cracks.
Place on a greased baking tray and bake at 180°C for 15-18 minutes until golden.
Meanwhile, boil sugar and water with cardamom to make syrup.
Soak the baked balls in warm syrup for at least 1 hour before serving.
notes: Baking instead of frying significantly reduces the oil content while keeping them soft.

name: Mini Gulab Jamun Light
preparation: Mix milk powder, maida, and ghee. Add milk to form a soft, smooth dough.
Roll into very small marble-sized balls, ensuring no cracks.
Deep fry on very low heat until deep golden brown all over.
Prepare sugar syrup with water, sugar, and cardamom.
Soak the mini jamuns in warm syrup for at least 1 hour.
notes: Mini size means quicker frying and better syrup absorption throughout.

name: Kaju Katli Light
preparation: Grind cashews to a very fine powder without releasing oils.
Make sugar syrup by boiling sugar and water to single-string consistency.
Add cashew powder and cardamom to the syrup, stir continuously on low heat.
Cook until the mixture forms a non-sticky dough that pulls away from the pan.
Roll between parchment paper to thin sheet, apply silver vark, and cut into diamond shapes.
notes: Grind cashews in short pulses to avoid releasing oils and making a paste.

name: Sugar Free Kaju Katli
preparation: Grind cashews to a fine powder in short pulses.
Dissolve sugar-free sweetener in water and heat to a syrup consistency.
Add cashew powder and cardamom, stir on low heat until it forms a dough.
Roll out between parchment sheets to desired thickness.
Cool and cut into diamond shapes.
notes: Perfect for diabetics who want to enjoy traditional Indian sweets.

name: Kaju Pista Roll Light
preparation: Grind cashews and pistachios separately into fine powders.
Make sugar syrup by boiling sugar and water to one-string consistency.
Add cashew powder and cardamom to the syrup, stir on low heat until it forms a pliable dough.
Roll out the cashew dough into a thin rectangle, spread pistachio powder evenly over it.
Roll up tightly like a Swiss roll, refrigerate for 1 hour, then slice into rounds.
notes: Chill the roll before slicing for clean, neat round cuts.

name: Petha Light
ingredients: 1/2 cups ash gourd (petha), peeled and cubed
preparation: Peel and cut ash gourd into 1-inch cubes, prick them all over with a fork.
Dissolve a pinch of alum in water, soak the ash gourd cubes for 2 hours, then rinse well.
Boil the cubes in water until they turn translucent but still hold their shape.
Prepare sugar syrup with sugar, water, and cardamom powder.
Add the translucent pieces to the syrup and cook on low heat until they absorb the syrup and turn glossy.
notes: Soaking in alum water gives petha its characteristic translucent and crunchy texture.

name: Agra Petha Low Sugar
ingredients: 1/2 cups ash gourd, peeled and cubed
preparation: Peel and cube the ash gourd and prick each piece with a fork.
Soak pieces in alum water for 2 hours, then drain and rinse thoroughly.
Boil the cubes in plain water until they become translucent, then drain.
Make a light sugar syrup with reduced sugar, water, and cardamom.
Simmer the ash gourd pieces in the syrup on low heat until they absorb the flavoring.
notes: Reducing sugar by half still yields a pleasantly sweet petha with fewer calories.

name: Angoori Petha Light
preparation: Scoop small grape-sized balls from ash gourd using a melon baller, prick with a fork.
Soak in alum water for 2 hours, then rinse and drain.
Boil the balls in water until translucent, then drain gently.
Prepare light sugar syrup with sugar, water, cardamom, and rose water.
Add the translucent balls to the syrup and cook on low flame until they absorb the syrup.
notes: The grape-like shape gives angoori petha its name - a delightful bite-sized treat.

name: Modak Steamed
preparation: Cook grated coconut and jaggery on low heat until jaggery melts and mixture is dry. Add cardamom.
Boil water with ghee and salt, add rice flour, stir quickly, cover and rest 5 minutes.
Knead the rice dough until smooth and pliable. Divide into portions.
Flatten each portion into a cup, fill with coconut-jaggery mixture, and pinch edges to form modak shape.
Steam for 10-12 minutes until the dough turns translucent.
notes: Keep dough covered with a damp cloth while shaping to prevent drying out.

name: Ukadiche Modak
preparation: Cook grated coconut and jaggery on low heat, stirring until dry. Add cardamom.
Boil water with ghee, add rice flour, stir vigorously, cover for 2 minutes, then knead smooth.
Take a small ball, flatten into a thin cup shape using thumb and fingers.
Place filling in center, bring edges together, and pinch to form pleats, sealing at top.
Steam in a greased steamer for 10-15 minutes until the outer covering becomes shiny.
notes: Ukadiche means steamed in Marathi - the traditional Ganesh Chaturthi preparation.

name: Dry Fruit Modak
preparation: Crumble khoya and cook on low heat until it melts and turns slightly golden.
Add jaggery powder and stir until dissolved and well combined.
Mix in chopped almonds, cashews, pistachios, and cardamom.
Remove from heat and cool until warm enough to handle.
Shape into modak shapes using a modak mould or by hand. Let set for 30 minutes.
notes: A modak mould makes shaping much easier and gives a professional finish.

name: Puran Poli Light
preparation: Pressure cook chana dal with turmeric until very soft, then drain all water.
Mash the dal, add jaggery, and cook on low heat until the puran filling is thick and dry.
Add cardamom to the filling and cool completely.
Knead whole wheat flour into a soft dough. Stuff each ball with puran, seal, and roll out thin.
Cook on a hot tawa with a little ghee on both sides until golden brown.
notes: Using whole wheat flour instead of maida adds fiber for a healthier version.

name: Holige Light
preparation: Cook toor dal with turmeric until very soft, drain excess water.
Add jaggery to the hot dal and cook on low heat until thick and dry.
Add cardamom to the filling and cool completely.
Make a soft dough with whole wheat flour. Stuff, seal, and roll out into thin discs.
Cook on a hot tawa with ghee until golden brown on both sides.
notes: Holige is the Karnataka version of puran poli, traditionally made during festivals.

name: Obbattu Light
preparation: Cook chana dal with turmeric until very soft, drain, and mash smooth.
Cook mashed dal with jaggery and cardamom until thick and dry.
Prepare a soft dough with whole wheat flour and rest for 15 minutes.
Take dough portion, stuff with filling, close edges, and roll out into a thin disc.
Roast on a hot tawa with minimal ghee until golden on both sides.
notes: Obbattu is the South Indian name for the same beloved stuffed flatbread dessert.

name: Basundi Light
preparation: Boil low-fat milk in a heavy-bottomed pan on medium heat.
Reduce heat and simmer, stirring frequently, until the milk reduces to half.
Add sugar, cardamom, and saffron; stir until dissolved.
Continue simmering until slightly thick and creamy.
Add chopped nuts and serve warm or chilled.
notes: Scraping milk solids from pan sides and mixing back gives basundi its characteristic grainy texture.

name: Rabri Light
preparation: Boil low-fat milk in a wide, heavy-bottomed pan.
As cream forms on top, push it to the side with a spatula. Repeat multiple times.
Continue until the milk reduces to about one-third.
Add sugar, saffron, and cardamom; stir gently without breaking cream layers.
Cool, chill in refrigerator, and serve topped with sliced almonds.
notes: Use a wide pan to increase surface area for faster evaporation and more cream layers.

name: Malai Rabri Light
preparation: Pour milk into a wide pan and bring to a boil.
Let cream layer form, push to the side. Repeat 8-10 times as milk reduces.
Continue reducing until one-third of original volume with thick consistency.
Add sugar, saffron, cardamom, and kewra water. Stir gently.
Cool, refrigerate 2-3 hours, and serve chilled with chopped pistachios.
notes: Kewra water adds an authentic North Indian fragrance that elevates the rabri.

name: Atte Ka Halwa Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a heavy pan and add whole wheat flour.
Roast on low heat for 10-12 minutes until deep brown with rich aroma.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and strain.
Slowly add jaggery water to roasted atta while stirring vigorously.
Cook until halwa thickens and leaves the pan sides. Garnish with almonds.
notes: Patient roasting is the key - atta should turn rich brown for the best flavor.

name: Besan Halwa Light
preparation: Heat ghee and add besan to the pan.
Roast on low flame for 8-10 minutes until golden and aromatic.
Dissolve sugar in warm water.
Carefully pour the sweetened water into besan while stirring vigorously.
Add cardamom and cook until halwa thickens and releases ghee. Garnish with cashews.
notes: Besan must be roasted until fragrant to eliminate the raw taste completely.

name: Chana Dal Halwa Light
preparation: Soak chana dal for 2 hours, drain, and grind into a coarse paste.
Heat ghee and add the ground dal paste.
Roast on low heat for 12-15 minutes until golden and fragrant.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and add to the dal, stirring well.
Cook until halwa thickens and pulls away from pan. Add cardamom and garnish with almonds.
notes: Soaking the dal ensures it grinds easily and cooks faster for a smoother halwa.

name: Malpua Light
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour, semolina, and milk to form a smooth pouring-consistency batter.
Add cardamom and rest the batter for 30 minutes.
Heat a little oil in a flat pan and pour a small ladle of batter into a disc.
Shallow fry on medium heat until golden brown on both sides.
Make a light syrup with sugar and water, dip each malpua briefly, and serve warm.
notes: Shallow frying instead of deep frying makes this a lighter version of traditional malpua.

name: Mini Malpua Healthy
preparation: Mash banana and mix with whole wheat flour, milk, and cardamom to make a smooth batter.
Let the batter rest for 15 minutes.
Heat a non-stick tawa with a tiny amount of ghee.
Pour small spoonfuls to make mini pancake-sized malpuas, cook until golden on both sides.
Serve warm with a drizzle of honey if desired.
notes: Mashed banana adds natural sweetness and binding, reducing the need for sugar.

name: Oats Malpua
preparation: Powder the oats in a mixer and combine with whole wheat flour.
Add milk, jaggery powder, and cardamom to make a smooth batter.
Rest the batter for 20 minutes to thicken slightly.
Heat a non-stick pan with a little ghee and pour small rounds of batter.
Cook on medium heat until golden brown on both sides. Serve warm.
notes: Oats add fiber and make this malpua more filling and nutritious.

name: Tilkut
preparation: Dry roast sesame seeds on low heat until golden and they start to pop.
Coarsely crush half the seeds using a mortar and pestle, keep the rest whole.
Melt jaggery in a pan with a teaspoon of water to form a sticky syrup.
Quickly add roasted sesame seeds and cardamom to the jaggery and mix well.
Grease palms with ghee and shape the warm mixture into flat, round tilkut discs.
notes: Tilkut is a traditional Makar Sankranti treat; sesame seeds are rich in calcium and iron.

name: Til Patti Light
preparation: Dry roast sesame seeds on low heat until lightly golden and aromatic.
Melt jaggery with a few drops of water on low flame until smooth syrup forms.
Test the syrup by dropping a bit in water; it should form a soft ball.
Add roasted sesame seeds and cardamom to the syrup, mix quickly.
Pour onto a greased surface, spread thin using a rolling pin, and cut into rectangles before it hardens.
notes: Work quickly once the syrup is ready as the mixture hardens fast.

name: Sesame Chikki Light
preparation: Dry roast sesame seeds on low flame until light golden, popping, and aromatic.
Melt jaggery in a non-stick pan on low heat until it forms a smooth liquid syrup.
Check consistency by dropping syrup in cold water; it should form a hard crack.
Add roasted seeds and a pinch of salt, mix thoroughly.
Quickly pour onto a greased tray, flatten with a greased rolling pin, and cut while warm.
notes: A tiny pinch of salt enhances the sweetness and brings out the nutty sesame flavor.

name: Mixed Nut Chikki
preparation: Roast almonds, cashews, and peanuts separately until golden.
Melt jaggery on low heat until smooth liquid syrup forms.
Test syrup consistency by dropping in cold water; it should crack.
Add all roasted nuts and mix quickly.
Pour onto a greased tray, flatten, and cut into bars while warm.
notes: The mix of different nuts provides a variety of textures and nutrients.

name: Seeds Chikki
preparation: Dry roast all seeds separately on low heat until lightly golden.
Melt jaggery with a few drops of water to form a smooth syrup.
Test syrup by dropping in cold water; it should form a hard ball.
Add all roasted seeds and mix thoroughly.
Spread onto a greased surface, press flat, and cut into squares while warm.
notes: Seeds are packed with omega-3 fatty acids and minerals, making this a superfood chikki.

name: Gond Ke Ladoo Light
preparation: Fry gond in a tsp of ghee until it puffs up and becomes crispy. Crush coarsely.
Roast whole wheat flour in remaining ghee on low heat until golden and aromatic.
Remove from heat and let it cool slightly.
Add powdered jaggery, crushed gond, cardamom, and chopped almonds. Mix well.
Shape into round ladoos while the mixture is still warm.
notes: Gond ladoos are traditionally eaten in winter for warmth and energy.

name: Methi Ladoo Light
preparation: Dry roast methi seeds on low heat until golden and aromatic, then grind to a fine powder.
Roast whole wheat flour in ghee on low heat until golden brown.
Remove from heat, add powdered jaggery, methi powder, cardamom, and coconut.
Mix well until everything is combined.
Shape into round ladoos while the mixture is warm.
notes: Methi ladoos are great for bone health and are traditionally given to new mothers.

name: Ajwain Ladoo
preparation: Dry roast ajwain seeds lightly until aromatic.
Roast whole wheat flour in ghee on low heat until golden brown and fragrant.
Remove from heat and cool slightly.
Add grated jaggery, roasted ajwain, and cardamom. Mix well.
Shape into round ladoos and let them set.
notes: Ajwain aids digestion and adds a unique flavor to these traditional winter ladoos.

name: Atta Biscuit Healthy
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour with ghee until crumbly.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom, mix well.
Add milk gradually to form a firm dough; do not over-knead.
Roll out to 1/4 inch thickness and cut into shapes.
Bake at 170°C for 18-20 minutes until golden. Cool completely before eating.
notes: These biscuits become crunchier as they cool down completely.

name: Ragi Biscuit
preparation: Mix ragi flour and whole wheat flour together.
Rub in ghee until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Add powdered jaggery and mix. Add milk to form a firm dough.
Roll out and cut into round shapes.
Bake at 170°C for 15-18 minutes until firm. Cool on a wire rack.
notes: Ragi is rich in calcium, making these biscuits great for bone health.

name: Oats Biscuit
preparation: Powder the oats and mix with whole wheat flour.
Rub ghee into the flour mixture until crumbly.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and enough milk to form a firm dough.
Roll out and cut into desired shapes.
Bake at 170°C for 15-18 minutes until golden. Cool before serving.
notes: Adding oats increases fiber content, keeping you fuller for longer.

name: Moong Dal Barfi Light
preparation: Soak moong dal for 2 hours, drain, and grind to a coarse paste.
Roast the dal paste in ghee on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring continuously until golden.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom, mixing well.
Cook until the mixture thickens and starts leaving the sides.
Spread onto a greased plate, garnish with pistachios, cool, and cut into squares.
notes: Roast the dal paste patiently; rushing causes uneven cooking and raw taste.

name: Besan Barfi Light
preparation: Roast besan in ghee on low heat for 10-12 minutes until golden and aromatic.
Remove from heat and let it cool for 2 minutes.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom, mix quickly until well combined.
Press the mixture firmly into a greased tray.
Garnish with sliced almonds, let it set, and cut into diamonds.
notes: Add sugar while the mixture is still warm for the best texture.

name: Coconut Peda
preparation: Cook grated coconut and crumbled khoya together on low heat for 5 minutes.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom, stir continuously.
Cook until the mixture becomes thick and leaves the pan sides.
Cool slightly and shape into flat round peda shapes.
Press a piece of pistachio on top of each peda.
notes: Use fresh coconut for the best flavor; desiccated coconut works but is less moist.

name: Milk Peda Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a non-stick pan, add milk powder and stir.
Add low-fat milk gradually, stirring continuously to avoid lumps.
Add sugar and cardamom, cook on low heat until the mixture thickens.
Once it forms a dough-like consistency, remove from heat and cool slightly.
Shape into flat round pedas and press a design on top with a fork.
notes: Do not overcook or the pedas will become hard; they should be soft and melt-in-mouth.

name: Kesar Peda Light
preparation: Soak saffron strands in 1 tbsp warm milk for 10 minutes.
Heat ghee, add milk powder, and stir. Add milk gradually.
Add sugar, saffron milk, and cardamom; cook on low heat until thick.
When it forms a dough, remove from heat and cool slightly.
Shape into flat round pedas and garnish with a saffron strand on top.
notes: Use genuine Kashmiri saffron for the best color and aroma.

name: Kalakand Light
preparation: Boil milk and add lemon juice to curdle. Strain through muslin to get soft chhena.
Do not press too hard; chhena should remain moist and soft.
Cook chhena with sugar on low heat, stirring gently.
Add cardamom and cook until the mixture becomes semi-solid and grainy.
Spread in a greased tray, garnish with pistachios, cool, and cut into squares.
notes: The grainy, crumbly texture is the hallmark of good kalakand.

name: Chhena Poda Light
preparation: Knead chhena well until smooth and creamy.
Add sugar, cardamom, semolina, and raisins; mix thoroughly.
Caramelize 1 tbsp sugar in a baking tin until golden.
Pour the chhena mixture over the caramelized sugar.
Bake at 180°C for 30-35 minutes until the top is golden brown. Cool and slice.
notes: Chhena Poda is a famous Odia dessert meaning 'burnt cheese' - the caramelization is key.

name: Rajbhog Light
preparation: Knead chhena with maida until very smooth.
Add a little saffron and cardamom to a small portion of chhena for the stuffing.
Take larger portions, flatten, place stuffing inside, and shape into smooth large balls.
Boil sugar and water for syrup, gently add the balls, and cook for 15-18 minutes.
Let them cool in the syrup. Serve at room temperature or chilled.
notes: Rajbhog is essentially a larger rasgulla stuffed with saffron and dry fruits.

name: Baked Jalebi Light
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour, yogurt, and baking soda into a smooth batter. Rest for 4 hours to ferment.
Make sugar syrup with sugar, water, and saffron; boil to a sticky consistency.
Pour batter into a squeeze bottle and pipe spiral shapes onto a greased baking tray.
Bake at 200°C for 10-12 minutes until golden and crispy.
Dip the baked jalebis into warm sugar syrup for 30 seconds and serve immediately.
notes: Baking instead of frying drastically reduces oil content while keeping jalebis crispy.

name: Mini Imarti Light
preparation: Soak urad dal for 4 hours, drain, and grind to a smooth, thick batter. Add turmeric.
Make sugar syrup with sugar, water, and saffron; keep warm.
Fill the batter in a squeeze bottle. Heat oil in a flat pan.
Pipe small flower-shaped spirals into the oil and fry on low heat until crispy.
Drain and soak in warm sugar syrup for 1-2 minutes. Serve warm.
notes: Mini imartis absorb syrup better and are more crispy than larger ones.

name: Sandesh Bengali Light
preparation: Knead chhena for 8-10 minutes until very smooth and no graininess remains.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom; knead again until well combined.
Cook the mixture on low heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring continuously.
Remove from heat, cool slightly, and shape into desired forms using moulds.
Garnish with chopped pistachios and refrigerate before serving.
notes: The quality of chhena determines the sandesh - use fresh, soft chhena for the best results.

name: Nolen Gur Sandesh
preparation: Knead chhena until very smooth and silky.
Cook on low heat for 2-3 minutes while stirring.
Add nolen gur and cardamom; mix quickly off the heat to preserve the gur's delicate flavor.
Shape into sandesh moulds or small round discs.
Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
notes: Add nolen gur after removing from heat to preserve its delicate caramel notes.

name: Bhapa Sandesh
preparation: Knead chhena until completely smooth.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and saffron; mix well.
Pour into a greased steel container or mould.
Steam in a steamer or pressure cooker (without whistle) for 15-20 minutes.
Cool completely, unmould, garnish with pistachios, and slice to serve.
notes: Bhapa means steamed in Bengali; steaming gives a unique soft, pudding-like texture.

name: Mango Basundi Light
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to half on low heat, stirring frequently.
Add sugar and saffron; stir until dissolved.
Let the reduced milk cool to room temperature.
Mix in fresh mango pulp and cardamom.
Refrigerate for 2 hours and serve chilled.
notes: Add mango pulp only after the milk has cooled to prevent curdling.

name: Sitaphal Basundi Light
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to half on low heat, stirring often.
Add sugar and cardamom; stir until dissolved and let it cool.
Remove custard apple seeds and mash the pulp.
Mix the sitaphal pulp into the cooled reduced milk.
Garnish with almonds, chill for 2 hours, and serve cold.
notes: Sitaphal basundi is a Maharashtrian specialty made during custard apple season.

name: Apple Rabdi Light
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to one-third on low heat, stirring frequently.
Add sugar and cardamom, stir until dissolved.
Grate the apple and add to the thickened milk.
Add cinnamon powder and cook for 3-4 more minutes.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: Cinnamon pairs wonderfully with apple in this unique fusion rabdi.

name: Mixed Fruit Rabdi
ingredients: 1/12 cup mixed fruits (mango, pomegranate, apple), chopped
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to one-third on low heat, stirring frequently.
Add sugar, saffron, and cardamom; stir until dissolved.
Cool the rabdi to room temperature, then chill.
Fold in chopped mixed fruits just before serving.
Serve chilled in bowls.
notes: Add fruits just before serving to keep them fresh and prevent the rabdi from thinning.

name: Date Palm Jaggery Pudding
preparation: Dry roast semolina until lightly golden.
Boil milk and add the roasted semolina, stirring to avoid lumps.
Cook on low heat until the mixture thickens to pudding consistency.
Remove from heat and add nolen gur and cardamom; stir well.
Garnish with chopped cashews and serve warm or chilled.
notes: Nolen gur has a unique smoky caramel flavor that regular jaggery cannot replicate.

name: Patishapta Light
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour, rice flour, and milk to make a thin crepe batter.
For filling, cook grated coconut with jaggery on low heat until thick and sticky.
Heat a non-stick pan with a tiny bit of ghee and pour a thin layer of batter.
Cook the crepe on one side until edges lift, then flip briefly.
Place filling along one edge and roll the crepe tightly. Serve warm.
notes: Patishapta is a Bengali crepe dessert traditionally made during Makar Sankranti.

name: Pithe Light
preparation: Mix grated coconut, jaggery, and cardamom to prepare the sweet filling.
Knead rice flour with warm water to make a soft, pliable dough.
Take small balls of dough and flatten into thin circles.
Place filling in the center, fold into half-moons, and seal edges with a fork.
Steam for 12-15 minutes until the dough turns translucent.
notes: Steamed pithas are much lighter than fried versions and equally delicious.

name: Sheer Khurma Light
ingredients: 1 tsp mixed dry fruits (almonds, pistachios, cashews)
preparation: Lightly roast vermicelli in a tsp of ghee until golden brown.
Boil milk and add the roasted vermicelli.
Simmer on low heat until vermicelli is cooked and milk thickens.
Add sugar, cardamom, and saffron; stir until dissolved.
Add sliced dry fruits and serve warm or chilled.
notes: Sheer Khurma is a festive Eid special; roasting vermicelli adds a rich nutty flavor.

name: Seviyan Kheer Light
preparation: Roast seviyan in a tsp of ghee until golden.
Boil milk separately and add the roasted seviyan.
Simmer on low heat until seviyan softens and kheer thickens.
Add sugar, cardamom, and raisins; cook for 2-3 more minutes.
Serve warm or chilled as preferred.
notes: Break seviyan into smaller pieces before roasting for a more even texture.

name: Badam Burfi Light
preparation: Blanch almonds in hot water for 10 minutes, peel, and grind to a fine paste.
Cook the almond paste in a non-stick pan on low heat with milk, stirring constantly.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and saffron soaked in warm milk.
Stir until the mixture thickens and pulls away from the pan.
Spread onto a greased plate, cool, and cut into diamond shapes.
notes: Blanching and peeling almonds gives a smoother, whiter burfi with better texture.

name: Pista Burfi Light
preparation: Grind pistachios to a fine powder without over-processing into paste.
Cook pistachio powder with milk in a non-stick pan on low heat.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and saffron; stir continuously.
Cook until the mixture forms a thick dough that leaves the pan sides.
Transfer to a greased tray, press evenly, cool, and cut into squares.
notes: Use raw unsalted pistachios for natural green color and authentic sweet flavor.

name: Cashew Date Roll
preparation: Soak dates in warm water for 15 minutes, then drain and blend into a smooth paste.
Coarsely chop roasted cashews for some crunch.
Mix date paste with chopped cashews and cardamom.
Shape into a log on plastic wrap and roll in desiccated coconut.
Refrigerate for 1 hour, then slice into rounds.
notes: Dates provide natural sweetness, eliminating the need for added sugar.

name: Churma Ladoo Light
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour with 1 tbsp ghee and water to make a stiff dough.
Shape into small discs and bake at 180°C for 15-18 minutes until golden.
Cool the baked discs and crush them coarsely.
Add remaining melted ghee, powdered jaggery, cardamom, and almonds.
Mix well and shape into round ladoos.
notes: Baking instead of deep frying is the key to making churma ladoo healthier.

name: Mohanthal Light
preparation: Mix besan with 1 tbsp ghee and a few drops of milk to create a crumbly texture.
Pass through a sieve to create uneven granules.
Roast granules in remaining ghee for 10-12 minutes until golden.
Remove from heat, add sugar, cardamom, and milk; mix thoroughly.
Press firmly into a greased tray, garnish with almonds, let set, and cut into diamonds.
notes: Creating crumbly besan before roasting gives Mohanthal its characteristic grainy bite.

name: Sukhdi Light
preparation: Heat ghee in a heavy pan and add whole wheat flour.
Roast on low heat for 8-10 minutes until fragrant and golden.
Add grated jaggery and cardamom, mixing quickly as jaggery melts.
Stir vigorously until smooth and homogeneous.
Pour into a greased plate, spread evenly, score into squares while warm, and cool.
notes: Sukhdi is a Gujarati whole wheat fudge; it stays fresh for up to a week.

name: Mysore Pak Light
preparation: Prepare sugar syrup by boiling sugar and water to single-string consistency.
In a separate pan, melt ghee and keep warm on very low heat.
Slowly add besan to the sugar syrup while stirring vigorously.
Alternately add spoonfuls of warm ghee while stirring on medium-low heat.
Cook until the mixture becomes porous and ghee separates. Pour into a greased tray and cut.
notes: Work quickly as the mixture sets fast; the light version uses less ghee.

name: Boondi Ladoo Light
preparation: Make a smooth besan batter with water to dropping consistency.
Heat oil and pour batter through a perforated ladle to form small boondis.
Fry until golden and crisp, drain on paper towels.
Prepare one-string sugar syrup with cardamom.
Add warm boondis and raisins to syrup, mix, and shape into ladoos while warm.
notes: Use minimal oil and drain well; boondis should be slightly soft for easy shaping.

name: Motichoor Ladoo Light
preparation: Make a thin, flowing besan batter.
Heat oil and pour batter through a fine perforated ladle for very tiny boondis.
Fry briefly until just cooked but still soft; drain immediately.
Prepare sugar syrup with cardamom to one-string consistency.
Soak soft boondis in syrup for 10 minutes, squeeze gently, and shape into smooth round ladoos.
notes: The key difference from boondi ladoo is much smaller boondi size and softer, melt-in-mouth texture.

name: Rava Ladoo
preparation: Dry roast rava on low heat until slightly golden and nutty aroma develops.
Add desiccated coconut and toast for another minute.
Remove from heat and cool to room temperature.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, melted ghee, and raisins; mix well.
Take small portions and press firmly to shape into round ladoos.
notes: Roasting rava slowly on low heat is crucial for the right texture and flavor.

name: Dry Fruit Chikki
preparation: Roast almonds, cashews, pumpkin seeds, and sesame seeds separately until golden.
Melt jaggery in a heavy pan on low heat until smooth liquid forms.
Test by dropping in water; it should crack and become brittle.
Add all roasted dry fruits and seeds, mix quickly.
Pour onto a greased surface, flatten, and cut into bars while warm.
notes: Work quickly once the jaggery reaches the right stage as it hardens fast.

name: Honey Nut Ladoo
preparation: Roast chopped almonds and cashews lightly in ghee until golden.
Add powdered oats and roast for another 2 minutes on low heat.
Remove from heat and cool slightly.
Add honey and cardamom, mixing until well combined.
Shape into small round ladoos while warm. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.
notes: Honey adds natural sweetness and helps bind the ladoos without refined sugar.

name: Mawa Cake Light
preparation: Mix whole wheat flour and baking powder in a bowl.
Crumble mawa finely and add to the flour mixture.
Add powdered sugar, melted butter, and milk; mix to form a smooth batter.
Pour into a greased and lined small baking tin.
Bake at 170°C for 25-30 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean. Cool before slicing.
notes: Mawa cake is a Parsi specialty; the khoya gives it a distinctive rich, dense crumb.

name: Nankhatai Light
preparation: Cream ghee and powdered sugar together until light.
Mix besan, whole wheat flour, baking soda, and cardamom separately.
Add dry ingredients to the creamed ghee and mix to form a soft dough.
Divide into small balls and flatten slightly on a lined baking tray.
Press a pistachio on each and bake at 170°C for 15-18 minutes. Cool on tray.
notes: The combination of besan and wheat flour gives nankhatai its signature crumbly texture.

name: Shrewsbury Biscuit Light
preparation: Cream butter and powdered sugar until light and fluffy.
Add yogurt and vanilla extract, beating until smooth.
Fold in whole wheat flour and baking powder to form a soft dough.
Chill for 30 minutes, then roll out to 1/4 inch thickness.
Cut into round shapes and bake at 180°C for 12-14 minutes until golden.
notes: Shrewsbury biscuits are a beloved Pune bakery classic; chilling the dough ensures clean cuts.

name: Banana Walnut Ladoo
preparation: Mash ripe bananas thoroughly until smooth.
Dry roast powdered oats for 2-3 minutes until slightly toasted.
Combine banana, oats, walnuts, honey, and cinnamon.
Mix well until evenly combined into a thick mixture.
Shape into small round balls and refrigerate for 1 hour until firm.
notes: Use very ripe bananas with brown spots for maximum natural sweetness.

name: Banana Date Balls
preparation: Blend dates into a smooth, sticky paste.
Mash bananas and combine with date paste.
Add powdered oats and cardamom, mixing until a thick dough forms.
Roll into small balls.
Roll each ball in desiccated coconut and refrigerate for 1-2 hours.
notes: These no-sugar-added balls get all their sweetness from bananas and dates.

name: Banana Oat Balls
preparation: Mash bananas until completely smooth.
Add rolled oats, peanut butter, honey, and cinnamon.
Mix thoroughly until well incorporated.
Refrigerate mixture for 15 minutes if too soft.
Roll into small bite-sized balls and refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
notes: A great pre-workout snack with a good balance of carbs and protein.

name: Jackfruit Payasam Light
preparation: Chop ripe jackfruit into small pieces and cook in a little water until soft.
Mash the cooked jackfruit lightly.
Add light coconut milk and simmer on low heat for 5 minutes.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water, strain, and add to the payasam.
Add cardamom, garnish with fried cashews, and serve warm.
notes: Jackfruit payasam is a traditional Kerala festival dessert with a unique fruity sweetness.

name: Banana Payasam Light
preparation: Slice bananas into rounds and cook in a little ghee until golden.
Add light coconut milk and simmer on low heat.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water, strain, and add to the mixture.
Add cardamom and cook for 5 more minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee, garnish and serve warm.
notes: Nendran bananas give the most authentic flavor; regular bananas work too.

name: Pineapple Payasam Light
preparation: Cook chopped pineapple in a pan until soft and slightly caramelized.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and strain.
Add jaggery syrup to the cooked pineapple and mix well.
Add coconut milk and cardamom; simmer on low heat for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews in ghee, garnish and serve warm or chilled.
notes: The tangy pineapple balances the sweetness of jaggery beautifully.

name: Apple Payasam
preparation: Boil milk and reduce slightly on low heat.
Grate apples and add to the milk.
Add sugar, cardamom, and raisins.
Cook for 5-7 minutes until the apple softens and flavors meld.
Serve warm or chilled as preferred.
notes: Use sweet apple varieties like Fuji or Gala for the best natural sweetness.

name: Mixed Fruit Payasam
ingredients: 1/6 cup mixed fruits (apple, banana, mango), chopped
preparation: Cook chopped fruits lightly in a pan for 2-3 minutes.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and strain.
Add jaggery syrup and coconut milk to the fruits.
Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes, add cardamom.
Garnish with fried cashews and serve warm.
notes: Use seasonal fruits for the best flavor and nutritional value.

name: Chana Dal Payasam
preparation: Pressure cook chana dal until soft, then mash lightly.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and strain.
Add jaggery syrup to the cooked dal and mix well.
Add coconut milk and cardamom; simmer on low for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee, garnish and serve.
notes: Chana dal adds protein, making this payasam more nutritious than rice-based versions.

name: Moong Dal Payasam
preparation: Dry roast moong dal until golden, then pressure cook until very soft.
Mash the cooked dal lightly.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water and add to the mashed dal.
Add coconut milk and cardamom; simmer for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews in ghee and garnish. Serve warm.
notes: Dry roasting the dal before cooking adds a richer, nuttier flavor to the payasam.

name: Semiya Payasam Light
preparation: Roast vermicelli in a tsp of ghee until golden.
Cook in a little water until soft.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water, strain, and add to the cooked semiya.
Add coconut milk and cardamom; simmer for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee and garnish.
notes: This is the coconut milk version popular in South India, different from the milk-based seviyan kheer.

name: Tender Coconut Pudding
preparation: Blend tender coconut meat with coconut water until smooth.
Dissolve gelatin/agar-agar in warm milk.
Heat the coconut blend with sugar until sugar dissolves.
Add the gelatin mixture and stir well.
Pour into moulds and refrigerate for 4-5 hours until set.
notes: Use fresh tender coconut for the most delicate flavor; canned works but is less fragrant.

name: Coconut Cream Light
preparation: Mix cornstarch with 2 tbsp of coconut milk to make a slurry.
Heat remaining coconut milk with sugar until warm.
Add the cornstarch slurry and stir continuously.
Cook on low heat until the mixture thickens to a creamy consistency.
Add cardamom, pour into bowls, chill, and garnish with toasted coconut flakes.
notes: This is a lighter alternative to heavy cream-based desserts.

name: Coconut Chia
preparation: Dissolve jaggery in slightly warm coconut milk.
Add chia seeds and cardamom, stir well.
Refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight, stirring once after 30 minutes.
The chia seeds will absorb the liquid and form a pudding-like consistency.
Serve chilled, garnished with chopped pistachios.
notes: Stir once after 30 minutes to prevent chia seeds from clumping at the bottom.

name: Saffron Phirni Light
ingredients: 1/12 cup basmati rice, soaked and ground
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes and grind to a coarse paste.
Boil milk and add the ground rice paste, stirring continuously.
Cook on low heat until the mixture thickens to a creamy pudding.
Add sugar, saffron soaked in warm milk, and cardamom.
Pour into earthen bowls, garnish with almonds, and chill before serving.
notes: Serving in earthen bowls (shikoras) is traditional and enhances the flavor.

name: Rose Phirni
ingredients: 1/12 cup basmati rice, soaked and ground
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes and grind to a coarse paste.
Boil milk and add the ground rice paste, stirring constantly.
Cook on low heat until thickened.
Add sugar, cardamom, and rose water; stir well.
Pour into bowls, garnish with rose petals, and refrigerate for 2 hours.
notes: Add rose water after removing from heat to preserve its delicate fragrance.

name: Cardamom Phirni
ingredients: 1/12 cup basmati rice, soaked and ground
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes and grind to a coarse paste.
Boil milk and add the rice paste, stirring constantly.
Cook on low heat until the mixture thickens to pudding consistency.
Add sugar and cardamom powder; stir well.
Pour into serving bowls, garnish with pistachios, and chill before serving.
notes: Freshly ground cardamom has a much more potent flavor than pre-ground powder.

name: Sweet Pongal Light
preparation: Dry roast moong dal until golden, then cook rice and dal together until very soft.
Mash the cooked rice and dal mixture.
Dissolve jaggery in warm water, strain, and add to the mixture.
Add ghee and cardamom; cook on low heat for 5 minutes.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee and garnish.
notes: Sweet Pongal is a temple prasad staple in South India during Pongal festival.

name: Sakkarai Pongal Light
preparation: Roast moong dal until golden and cook with rice until mushy.
Melt jaggery in water, strain, and add to the rice-dal mixture.
Add ghee and cardamom, cook on low heat until well combined and thick.
Fry cashews in ghee until golden.
Garnish with fried cashews and serve warm.
notes: Sakkarai means sugar in Tamil; this version uses jaggery for a healthier twist.

name: Chakkara Pongal Light
preparation: Cook rice and roasted moong dal together until soft and mushy.
Dissolve jaggery in a little water and add to the cooked mixture.
Add ghee, cardamom, and dry ginger powder; stir well.
Cook on low heat for 5-7 minutes until thick.
Garnish with fried cashews and serve warm.
notes: Dry ginger powder (sonth) adds a warm, spicy note unique to this variant.

name: Unniyappam Light
preparation: Mix rice flour, mashed banana, and jaggery dissolved in a little water.
Add cardamom and coconut pieces to the batter.
Heat an unniyappam/appe pan with a little oil in each cavity.
Pour batter into each mould and cook on low heat.
Flip when the bottom turns golden and cook the other side. Serve warm.
notes: Unniyappam is a beloved Kerala snack served at temple festivals and Onam sadhya.

name: Neyyappam Light
preparation: Dissolve jaggery in warm water and strain.
Mix rice flour with jaggery syrup to make a smooth batter.
Add grated coconut and cardamom to the batter.
Heat a neyyappam/appe pan with a little ghee in each cavity.
Pour batter and cook on low heat, flipping when golden. Serve warm.
notes: Neyyappam literally means ghee appam; the ghee gives it a distinctive rich flavor.

name: Kozhukattai Sweet Light
preparation: Cook coconut and jaggery together on low heat until thick for the filling.
Add cardamom to the filling and set aside.
Boil water, add rice flour, and stir quickly. Knead into a smooth dough.
Flatten small portions, fill with coconut-jaggery mixture, and shape into dumplings.
Steam for 10-12 minutes until cooked through.
notes: Kozhukattai is the Tamil version of modak, traditionally offered to Lord Ganesha.

name: Paneer Kheer Light
preparation: Crumble paneer finely so there are no large lumps.
Boil milk and reduce slightly on low heat.
Add crumbled paneer and cook for 5-7 minutes on low heat.
Add sugar, saffron, and cardamom; stir well.
Serve warm or chilled, garnished with chopped nuts.
notes: Paneer adds a rich, creamy texture and extra protein to this kheer.

name: Chhena Kheer
preparation: Knead fresh chhena until smooth and shape into small balls.
Boil milk and reduce by one-fourth.
Gently add chhena balls to the simmering milk.
Add sugar, cardamom, and raisins; cook for 10 minutes on low heat.
Serve warm or chilled.
notes: The chhena balls soften in the milk, creating a delightful texture contrast.

name: Rasgulla Kheer
preparation: Boil milk and reduce to about two-thirds on low heat.
Add sugar, saffron, and cardamom; stir until dissolved.
Squeeze excess syrup from mini rasgullas.
Add rasgullas to the thickened milk and simmer for 5 minutes.
Serve warm or chilled with rasgullas in the creamy kheer.
notes: This Bengali-fusion dessert combines two classics into one indulgent bowl.

name: Mixed Fruit Cream Light
ingredients: 1/3 cup mixed fruits (apple, banana, grapes, pomegranate)
preparation: Wash and chop all fruits into bite-sized pieces.
Whip the low-fat cream until soft peaks form.
Fold honey and cardamom into the whipped cream.
Gently fold in the chopped fruits.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes and serve chilled.
notes: A classic Indian party dessert; use seasonal fruits for the best flavor.

name: Fruit Custard Light
ingredients: 1/3 cup mixed fruits (apple, banana, grapes, mango)
preparation: Mix custard powder with 1/4 cup cold milk to make a smooth paste.
Boil remaining milk with sugar.
Add the custard paste to the boiling milk, stirring continuously.
Cook until thick, add vanilla extract, and let it cool completely.
Fold in chopped fruits and refrigerate for 2 hours before serving.
notes: Let the custard cool completely before adding fruits to prevent them from getting mushy.

name: Chia Pudding Chocolate
preparation: Mix almond milk, cocoa powder, honey, and vanilla until smooth.
Add chia seeds and stir well.
Refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Stir once after 30 minutes to prevent clumping.
Serve chilled, topped with berries or dark chocolate shavings.
notes: Use unsweetened cocoa powder and adjust honey to control sweetness.

name: Chia Pudding Berry
preparation: Mix almond milk, honey, and vanilla.
Add chia seeds and stir well.
Refrigerate for 4 hours or overnight, stirring once after 30 minutes.
Mash half the berries and stir into the set pudding.
Top with remaining whole berries and serve chilled.
notes: Layer the berries for a beautiful purple swirl effect.

name: Chia Pudding Mango
preparation: Mix coconut milk, honey, and chia seeds.
Refrigerate for 4 hours or overnight, stirring once after 30 minutes.
Blend mango pulp until smooth.
Layer chia pudding and mango pulp in a glass.
Serve chilled with fresh mango chunks on top.
notes: The mango-coconut combination creates a tropical flavor perfect for summer.

name: Chia Pudding Vanilla
preparation: Mix almond milk, maple syrup, and vanilla extract.
Add chia seeds and stir well.
Refrigerate for 4 hours or overnight.
Stir once after 30 minutes to prevent clumping.
Serve chilled topped with sliced almonds.
notes: Use real vanilla extract, not artificial, for the best flavor.

name: Yogurt Parfait Berry
preparation: Layer Greek yogurt at the bottom of a glass.
Add a layer of mixed berries.
Drizzle honey over the berries.
Add another layer of yogurt and top with granola and chia seeds.
Serve immediately to keep the granola crunchy.
notes: Add granola just before serving to maintain its crunch.

name: Yogurt Parfait Tropical
preparation: Chop tropical fruits into small pieces.
Layer yogurt in a glass, then add tropical fruits.
Drizzle honey over the fruits.
Add another yogurt layer and top with coconut flakes and macadamia nuts.
Serve chilled.
notes: Toasted coconut flakes add extra crunch and a deeper coconut flavor.

name: Yogurt Parfait Granola
preparation: Layer Greek yogurt in a glass or bowl.
Add a layer of granola.
Drizzle honey and scatter dried cranberries.
Top with another layer of yogurt and mixed seeds.
Serve immediately for best texture.
notes: Homemade granola lets you control sugar and oil content for a healthier parfait.

name: Baked Apple Cinnamon
preparation: Core the apples and make a well in the center.
Mix honey, cinnamon, walnuts, and raisins.
Stuff the mixture into the apple centers.
Bake at 180°C for 25-30 minutes until apples are soft and golden.
Serve warm with a dollop of Greek yogurt if desired.
notes: Use firm, tart apple varieties like Granny Smith for the best baked apple results.

name: Baked Pear Honey
preparation: Halve the pears and scoop out the core.
Place cut-side up in a baking dish.
Drizzle with honey and lemon juice, sprinkle cinnamon.
Top with chopped almonds.
Bake at 180°C for 20-25 minutes until tender. Serve warm.
notes: Ripe but firm pears hold their shape best during baking.

name: Baked Peach
preparation: Halve and pit the peaches.
Place cut-side up in a baking dish.
Mix oat crumble with cinnamon and a tiny bit of butter.
Fill each peach half with the crumble mixture and drizzle with honey.
Bake at 190°C for 18-20 minutes until soft and golden.
notes: Peaches become sweeter and more fragrant when baked; serve with vanilla yogurt.

name: Dark Chocolate Avocado Mousse
preparation: Scoop avocado flesh and blend until completely smooth.
Add cocoa powder, honey, almond milk, and vanilla.
Blend until silky and creamy with no lumps.
Divide into serving glasses.
